examples.title: Forward Chaining Algorithm
examples.description: Data-driven inference starting from known facts
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ForwardChain(KB, Goal)
 // KB contains facts and Horn clause rules
 // Each rule: (B1 ∧ B2 ∧ ... ∧ Bn) → H
 
 Agenda ← InitialFacts(KB)
 Inferred ← empty set
 
 WHILE Agenda is not empty AND Goal not in Inferred DO
 Current ← Pop(Agenda)
 Add Current to Inferred
 
 FOR each rule R in KB WHERE Current matches premise of R
 NewFacts ← ApplyModusPonens(R, Current)
 FOR each fact f in NewFacts
 IF f not in Inferred AND f not in Agenda THEN
 Add f to Agenda
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN Goal in Inferred
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: FC starts with known facts in agenda. For each fact, it finds rules where the fact matches a premise and applies Modus Ponens to derive new facts. Continues until goal is derived or no new facts possible.

examples.title: Backward Chaining Algorithm
examples.description: Goal-driven inference working backwards from query
examples.code:
PROCEDURE BackwardChain(KB, Goal)
 // Recursive goal reduction
 
 IF Goal is a ground fact THEN
 IF Goal in KB THEN RETURN True
 ELSE RETURN False
 END IF
 
 // Find rules with Goal as head
 FOR each rule R in KB WHERE Head(R) = Goal
 // Try to prove all premises of R
 AllProved ← True
 FOR each premise p in Premises(R)
 IF NOT BackwardChain(KB, p) THEN
 AllProved ← False
 Break
 END IF
 END FOR
 IF AllProved THEN RETURN True
 END FOR
 
 RETURN False
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: BC is recursive. To prove a goal, it finds rules where the goal is the head and tries to prove all premises. Uses unification to match variables. Goal-driven, stops when goal is proven or no rules apply.

examples.title: Unification Algorithm
examples.description: Finding Most General Unifier (MGU) for two literals
examples.code:
FUNCTION Unify(x, y, θ)
 // θ is current substitution
 
 IF θ = failure THEN RETURN failure
 IF x = y THEN RETURN θ
 IF Variable(x) THEN RETURN UnifyVar(x, y, θ)
 IF Variable(y) THEN RETURN UnifyVar(y, x, θ)
 IF Compound(x) AND Compound(y) THEN
 IF Predicate(x) ≠ Predicate(y) THEN RETURN failure
 RETURN Unify(Args(x), Args(y), θ)
 IF List(x) AND List(y) THEN
 IF empty(x) AND empty(y) THEN RETURN θ
 RETURN Unify(Rest(x), Rest(y), Unify(First(x), First(y), θ))
 
 RETURN failure
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION UnifyVar(v, term, θ)
 IF v in θ THEN RETURN Unify(θ(v), term, θ)
 IF occurs_check(v, term) THEN RETURN failure
 RETURN θ ∪ {v/term}
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Unification recursively matches two terms. UnifyVar handles variable substitution with occurs-check to prevent infinite structures. Returns Most General Unifier (MGU) - the least specific substitution that makes terms equal.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is term size
